name: Lucky Thompson
abstract: Eli "Lucky" Thompson (June 16, 1924 – July 30, 2005) was an American jazz tenor and soprano saxophonist whose playing combined elements of swing and bebop. Although John Coltrane usually receives the most credit for bringing the soprano saxophone out of obsolescence in the early 1960s, Thompson (along with Steve Lacy) embraced the instru…

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Charlie Parker Memorial Concert

about_quotes.speaker: Pierre Boussaguet
about_quotes.evidence: Lucky Thompson is mentioned by the [INTERVIEWER] as part of a list of great tenor saxophone players (Coleman Hawkins, Don Byas, Lucky Thompson) to contextualize the sound of the musician being discussed. The [INTERVIEWEE] does not mention Lucky Thompson in their response.
about_quotes.doc_title: Pierre Boussaguet interviewed by Matthieu Jaffre, Aix en Provence, France, July 28, 2020

about_quotes.speaker: Howard McGhee

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Barefield
about_quotes.evidence: And we went into Cafe Society, and that's when Fletcher had a stroke down there, and then I took the band over for the finish of the engagement. Dick Vance was playing trumpet and me on clarinet and Lucky Thompson on tenor and Fletcher and Jimmy Crawford on drums

about_quotes.speaker: Earl Watkins
about_quotes.evidence: Lucky Thompson on tenor. Then I can’t remember the rest of the saxophone section. Mingus on the bass. And there I am sitting up there. And so we did several tunes for Black & White.
about_quotes.doc_title: Earl Watkins interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Francisco, California, August 7, 2002
